Consistency should not erase necessary hierarchy or state differences. A migration boundary may temporarily host two systems with explicit isolation.

search.title: Authentic Primary Media
search.summary: Primary imagery reveals the actual subject users need to inspect and includes correct crop, dimensions, loading, and alternative text.
search.body: Make media useful evidence rather than generic atmosphere or an unstable decorative layer. Inspect the subject, crop, loading behavior, reserved space, and text alternative at representative widths. Dark overlays and aggressive crops often hide the subject and reduce contrast. Abstract or atmospheric imagery is valid when detailed inspection is not a user need.

search.title: Verified Delivery Report
search.summary: The final report names executed checks, inspected viewports and states, material fixes, and any remaining limitations.
search.body: Give reviewers a factual account of confidence instead of a generic claim that quality checks passed. Separate UI/UX review, static inspection, existing project checks, rendered observations, automated evidence, and untested risk. Do not imply that a command, viewport, browser, or e2e test was inspected when it was not. A conceptual-only response should state that no implementation checks apply.

search.title: SaaS and Operations
search.summary: Quiet, repeatable workspaces for frequent tasks, scanning, comparison, triage, and stateful business workflows.
search.body: Prioritize speed, predictability, data clarity, and comprehensive states over decorative marketing composition. Use stable navigation and aligned controls for repeated actions. Expose loading, empty, permission, error, success, and stale states. Keep headings and chrome compact enough to preserve working space. Avoid oversized heroes, decorative card walls, and expressive motion that slows repeated work. Onboarding and empty states may use more editorial spacing while preserving clear actions.

search.title: Commerce and Product Detail
search.summary: Inspectable product media paired with essential configuration, trust, fulfillment, and purchase information.
search.body: Help users evaluate and act without hiding the product behind atmosphere or scattering key facts across decorative sections. Show the real product clearly in the first viewport. Keep price, availability, configuration, fulfillment, returns, and primary action legible. Preserve selected variants and validation through errors. Do not fabricate reviews, scarcity, ratings, or customer activity. Luxury editorial commerce may use more narrative pacing when purchase facts remain accessible.

search.title: Editorial and Portfolio
search.summary: Authored reading rhythm, distinctive composition, and high-quality media for stories, essays, work, and case studies.
search.body: Balance expressive identity with legible reading, truthful attribution, and robust narrow-screen ordering. Keep text measure, heading hierarchy, and captions readable. Use media crops and pacing that serve the narrative. Retain authorship, dates, credits, and navigation context. Expressive layout must not scramble DOM order or hide links and captions. Experimental art experiences may prioritize exploration when the user explicitly requests it.

search.title: Data and Analytics
search.summary: Decision-oriented views that preserve comparison, units, filters, freshness, provenance, and drill-down context.
search.body: Choose tables and charts from user questions instead of decorating dashboards with arbitrary metrics. State the decision or question each visualization supports. Show units, time range, filters, freshness, and no-data states. Provide labels and non-color distinctions with a table or text alternative where needed. Do not use 3D charts, truncated axes, or metric cards without decision context. A monitoring wall may optimize distant scanning over detailed interaction.

search.title: Mobile Application
search.summary: Platform-aware navigation, reachable actions, safe-area handling, keyboard resilience, and explicit gesture alternatives.
search.body: Design for touch, limited height, interruption, and platform conventions rather than compressing a desktop interface. Account for safe areas, software keyboard, and short landscape height. Keep primary controls reachable and targets appropriately sized. Provide explicit alternatives to drag and swipe outcomes. Desktop hover, dense multicolumn layouts, and browser-style chrome rarely transfer directly. Tablet layouts may introduce secondary columns when content and input mode support them.

search.title: Campaign and Brand
search.summary: Expressive first viewports that establish the literal offer or subject and reveal evidence of the next section.
search.body: Create brand impact without generic split heroes, abstract decoration, hidden subjects, or unsupported claims. Make the brand, offer, place, person, product, or object a first-viewport signal. Use authentic or clearly identified media that reveals the subject. Keep the next content band partially visible on representative screens. Do not put the hero message inside a floating card or use a generic text-media split by default. A restrained institutional campaign may prioritize trust and clarity over expressive composition.

search.title: Next.js App Router
search.summary: Use Server Components by default, narrow client boundaries, framework routing and metadata, and explicit static-generation contracts.
search.body: Keep UI implementations aligned with current Next.js architecture while avoiding hydration, rendering, and deployment mistakes. Keep interactive Client Components small and pass serializable data into them. Use framework layouts, links, metadata, loading, error, and not-found conventions. For static export, generate every dynamic path and avoid unsupported server features. Do not access browser globals during server rendering or add client boundaries to entire route trees without need. A heavily interactive editor may justify a larger client-owned island with explicit boundaries.

search.title: React Component System
search.summary: Model state explicitly, preserve semantic elements, keep component ownership local, and avoid unsafe HTML or unnecessary effects.
search.body: Produce maintainable UI behavior without over-abstracted components, duplicated state, or unsafe rendering escape hatches. Use native controls and controlled state only where the workflow needs it. Keep effects for synchronization with external systems, not derived render data. Render untrusted content as text or sanitized data, never directly through unsafe HTML. Generic components with many boolean props often hide unclear ownership and state combinations. A vetted rich-text pipeline may render sanitized HTML through a tightly controlled boundary.

search.title: Tailwind Utility CSS
search.summary: Use a shared token vocabulary, stable responsive rules, and extracted components only where repetition creates real ownership.
search.body: Keep utility-first styling coherent without arbitrary-value drift, unreadable class duplication, or premature wrapper components. Map recurring semantic colors, spacing, radii, and type roles to shared tokens. Keep responsive behavior near the component and verify class ordering remains readable. Extract repeated component structure, not one-off class lists. Frequent arbitrary values and duplicated state selectors indicate a missing token or primitive. A truly unique fixed-format element may require one explicit arbitrary constraint.

search.title: Plain HTML and CSS
search.summary: Prefer semantic document structure, progressive enhancement, modern layout primitives, and minimal JavaScript for stateful behavior.
search.body: Build durable interfaces without recreating browser controls or adding a framework where the product does not need one. Use landmarks, native controls, form labels, and logical heading structure. Use grid, flexbox, container constraints, and media queries for layout. Make content and core navigation useful before optional JavaScript enhancements. Custom select, dialog, tab, and menu implementations carry substantial accessibility requirements. A complex application may require a state framework once interaction ownership exceeds local enhancement.

search.title: Native Mobile UI
search.summary: Use platform components, navigation, safe-area behavior, dynamic type, accessibility APIs, and native feedback conventions.
search.body: Keep mobile interfaces familiar, reachable, and resilient across text sizes, input methods, and device geometry. Prefer platform controls and accessibility semantics before custom drawing. Test dynamic text, keyboard, safe areas, rotation, and screen-reader order. Use native navigation and feedback behavior unless the brand requirement clearly outweighs it. Web hover and breakpoint assumptions do not map directly to native application layout. Games and immersive media may use custom-rendered controls with equivalent accessibility paths.

search.title: Three.js Interactive 3D
search.summary: Use a proven 3D engine, stable full-bleed canvas geometry, explicit loading and fallback states, and measured input performance.
search.body: Make 3D the actual experience rather than a decorative preview while keeping the page usable when rendering or assets fail. Verify nonblank rendered pixels and correct framing at mobile and desktop sizes. Provide loading, error, reduced-motion, and non-WebGL fallback behavior. Keep keyboard, touch, pointer, resize, and visibility lifecycle behavior coherent. Do not place the primary 3D experience inside a decorative card or omit rendered framing checks when a render is practical. A small 3D inspector within an operational tool may use a bounded functional viewport.
